title: Getting Around
subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around New Zealand.

items.label: Book ahead for scenic routes
items.text: The TranzAlpine and Northern Explorer are popular tourist trains that book out months in advance, especially in summer. Book directly through KiwiRail.co.nz for best availability and prices.
Long-distance trains are slow and infrequent—the TranzAlpine takes 4.5 hours to cross the South Island and runs only once daily. Use them for the experience, not speed.

items.label: Limited network coverage
items.text: Train services only connect major cities and tourist routes, not regional areas. Most New Zealanders rely on buses or driving for inter-city travel.

items.label: Multi-trip passes available
items.text: KiwiRail Scenic Passes offer flexibility across trains and some bus services, useful if combining multiple journeys.

items.label: Auckland and Wellington have best networks
items.text: Auckland Transport and Metlink (Wellington) operate frequent, reliable bus systems. Most other cities have limited evening and weekend services.

items.label: Get a contactless card
items.text: Use AT HOP (Auckland), Snapper (Wellington), or Bee Card (Christchurch) for cheaper fares than cash. Tourist cards are available at visitor centres.
Buses are frequently late or don't show up, especially outside major cities. Check real-time tracking apps before heading to the stop.

items.label: InterCity is the main coach operator
items.text: InterCity connects most towns and cities with affordable long-distance bus travel. Book online at intercity.co.nz for discounts.

items.label: Student and backpacker discounts
items.text: Flashpacker and YHA card holders get 10-15% discounts on many bus operators.

sections.title: Taxis & Ride Apps [Uber, Zoomy, Local Firms]

items.text: Uber is unavailable outside Auckland and Wellington; use local taxi apps or phone taxis instead. Availability is limited in regional areas.

items.label: Zoomy is the national ride-share app
items.text: Zoomy operates in most cities as a local alternative to Uber with similar pricing. Download the app or book via their website.

items.label: Always use registered taxis or apps
items.text: Avoid unmarked taxis or informal ride offers. Use official services to ensure safety and regulated fares.
Taxi fares are unregulated and can vary wildly between companies—get a quote or use an app with transparent pricing before accepting a ride.

items.label: Book taxis in advance in small towns
items.text: Rural and provincial towns may have no taxis waiting on the street; ask your accommodation to book one for you.

items.label: Drive on the left
items.text: New Zealand drives on the left side of the road. If unfamiliar, take extra time to adjust, especially at roundabouts and motorway merges.
Speed cameras, especially on State Highway 1, issue heavy fines ($150–$600+) for speeding. Stick to posted limits strictly.

items.label: Rental car companies are competitive
items.text: Compare Hertz, Avis, Budget, and local operators like Ace and East Coast Car Rentals. Book in advance for better rates, especially in peak season.
Many roads are narrow, winding, and poorly lit; avoid driving after dark if unfamiliar with conditions. Fatal crashes often occur on rural roads.

items.label: Petrol stations are sparse in rural areas
items.text: Fill up in towns before long stretches, especially on the South Island. Some rural pumps have limited hours.

items.label: International driving permit recommended
items.text: While a valid home license works for short-term tourism, carrying an IDP makes rental and police interactions smoother.

items.label: Major hubs: Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch
items.text: Auckland Airport (AKL) is the largest and busiest international gateway. Most domestic flights connect through these three centres.

items.label: Domestic airlines: Air New Zealand and Jetstar
items.text: Air New Zealand is the national carrier with most routes; Jetstar offers budget fares but with stricter baggage limits. Book direct or through travel sites.
Baggage fees add up quickly with budget carriers—a second checked bag can cost $25–$40. Read terms carefully before booking.

items.label: Airport transport options
items.text: Use the Airbus shuttle, rental car, or ride-share to reach city centres. Taxis are pricey (often $50–$80 from major airports).

items.label: Allow 2+ hours for domestic flights
items.text: Check in early and arrive 2 hours before departure; queues during peak times (7–9 AM) are common.

items.label: Interislander Ferry: Wellington to Picton
items.text: The main North–South Island link operates multiple times daily. Crossing takes 3.5 hours; book through Interislander.co.nz.

items.label: Bluebridge is a cheaper alternative
items.text: Bluebridge also runs Wellington to Picton with longer sailing times (4.5 hours) but lower fares. Good for budget travellers.
Cook Strait ferries can be cancelled or delayed due to rough seas, especially in winter. Book refundable tickets if flexible on dates.

items.label: Regional ferries to islands
items.text: Services to Waiheke Island (Auckland), Great Barrier Island, and Stewart Island exist but run infrequently. Plan ahead and book early.

items.label: Bring a car or book one on arrival
items.text: Ferry crossings allow vehicles; transport costs $100–$200 depending on car size. Alternatively, rent a car after crossing.

items.label: Buy passes, not single tickets
items.text: Daily and weekly passes offer better value than individual journeys. In Auckland, a Day Pass costs $20 vs. $3.50 per single trip.

items.label: InterCity FlexiPass for long-distance buses
items.text: Pre-purchase pass hours (e.g., 20 or 50 hours) valid on InterCity routes across New Zealand. Flexible routing and no fixed dates.

items.label: Tourist passes may not save money
items.text: Check the math—multi-day tourist passes often cost more than individual tickets if you're only making a few journeys.
Some passes and discounts require proof of residency or age; always carry ID when claiming discounts.

items.label: Book in advance online for discounts
items.text: InterCity, KiwiRail, and ferry operators offer 10–30% discounts for early bookings vs. at-counter purchases.

items.label: Three, Vodafone, and Spark are main providers
items.text: Buy prepaid SIM cards at the airport or convenience stores for $10–$20, then load credit online. Three offers the best rural coverage.
Rural and remote areas have poor or no 4G coverage; don't rely solely on mobile data for navigation in these zones.

items.label: Consider a 7- or 14-day plan
items.text: Prepaid plans start at $15–$30 for several GB of data. Buy at Spark, Vodafone, or Three shops for the best rates.

items.label: Free WiFi is common but patchy
items.text: Cafes, libraries, and visitor centres offer free WiFi, but reliability varies. Don't count on it for time-sensitive bookings.

items.label: Download offline maps before trips
items.text: Use Google Maps or Maps.me offline mode, especially for road trips through regions with no signal.
Roaming charges from overseas SIM cards are very expensive; buying a local SIM is strongly recommended.
